section.title: Repentance and the Lament over Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: Told of Galileans slaughtered by Pilate, Jesus refuses to rank victims by guilt and instead presses the same summons on everyone: unless you repent, you too will perish. A barren fig tree is spared one more year of patient tending, a bent woman is freed on the Sabbath, and the kingdom is likened to a mustard seed and to leaven, small beginnings that fill the earth. The chapter closes as Jesus, resolute toward Jerusalem, weeps over the city that kills its prophets and refuses to be gathered.
section.thesis: The kingdom presses its call to repentance on every hearer, offers one more season of patient grace, frees the bound even on the Sabbath, grows unstoppably from small beginnings, and comes at last as the tears of a Savior over a city that would not be gathered.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the call to repentance; the barren fig tree; the bent woman set free; the mustard seed and the leaven; the narrow door; and the lament over Jerusalem.

units.label: A call to repentance
units.note: unless you repent, you too will perish

verses.text: At that time some of those present told Jesus about the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mixed with their sacrifices.
To this He replied, “Do you think that these Galileans were worse sinners than all the other Galileans, because they suffered this way?
No, I tell you. But unless you {{metanoeo|repent}}, you too will all perish.
Or those eighteen who were killed when the tower of Siloam collapsed on them: Do you think that they were more sinful than all the others living in Jerusalem?
No, I tell you. But unless you {{metanoeo|repent}}, you too will all perish.”

units.label: The barren fig tree
units.note: leave it alone again this year

verses.text: Then Jesus told this parable: “A man had a fig tree that was planted in his vineyard. He went to look for fruit on it but did not find any.

So he said to the keeper of the vineyard, ‘Look, for the past three years I have come to search for fruit on this fig tree and haven’t found any. Therefore cut it down! Why should it use up the soil?’
‘Sir,’ the man replied, ‘leave it alone again this year, until I dig around it and fertilize it.
If it bears fruit next year, fine. But if not, you can cut it down.’”

units.label: The bent woman set free
units.note: should she not be released on the Sabbath

verses.text: One Sabbath Jesus was teaching in one of the synagogues,
and a woman there had been disabled by a spirit for eighteen years. She was hunched over and could not stand up straight.
When Jesus saw her, He called her over and said, “Woman, you are set free from your disability.”
Then He placed His hands on her, and immediately she straightened up and began to glorify God.
But the synagogue leader was indignant that Jesus had healed on the Sabbath. “There are six days for work,” he told the crowd. “So come and be healed on those days and not on the Sabbath.”
“You hypocrites!” the Lord replied. “Does not each of you on the Sabbath untie his ox or donkey from the stall and lead it to water?
Then should not this daughter of Abraham, whom Satan has kept bound for eighteen long years, be released from her bondage on the Sabbath day?”
When Jesus said this, all His adversaries were humiliated. And the whole crowd rejoiced at all the glorious things He was doing.

units.label: The mustard seed and the leaven
units.note: small beginnings that fill the earth

verses.text: Then Jesus asked, “What is the {{basileia|kingdom}} of God like? To what can I compare it?
It is like a mustard seed that a man tossed into his garden. It grew and became a tree, and the birds of the air nested in its branches.”
Again He asked, “To what can I compare the kingdom of God?
It is like leaven that a woman took and mixed into three measures of flour, until all of it was leavened.”
Then Jesus traveled throughout the towns and villages, teaching as He made His way toward Jerusalem.
“Lord,” someone asked Him, “will only a few people be saved?” Jesus answered,
“Make every effort to enter through the {{stenos|narrow door}}. For many, I tell you, will try to enter and will not be able.
After the master of the house gets up and shuts the door, you will stand outside knocking and saying, ‘Lord, open the door for us.’ But he will reply, ‘I do not know where you are from.’
Then you will say, ‘We ate and drank with you, and you taught in our streets.’
And he will answer, ‘I tell you, I do not know where you are from. Depart from me, all you evildoers.’
There will be weeping and gnashing of teeth when you see Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, but you yourselves are thrown out.
People will come from east and west and north and south, and will recline at the table in the kingdom of God.
And indeed, some who are last will be first, and some who are first will be last.”

units.label: The lament over Jerusalem
units.note: how often I have longed to gather your children

verses.text: At that very hour, some Pharisees came to Jesus and told Him, “Leave this place and get away, because Herod wants to kill You.”
But Jesus replied, “Go tell that fox, ‘Look, I will keep driving out demons and healing people today and tomorrow, and on the third day I will {{teleioo|reach My goal}}.’
Nevertheless, I must keep going today and tomorrow and the next day, for it is not admissible for a prophet to perish outside of Jerusalem.

O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, who kills the prophets and stones those sent to her, how often I have longed to {{episynago|gather}} your children together as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, but you were unwilling!
Look, your house is left to you desolate. And I tell you that you will not see Me again until you say, ‘Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord.’”

metanoeo.gloss: To repent, literally to change one's mind, to turn the whole self around. Jesus refuses to treat disaster as a scoreboard of guilt and turns the question back on His hearers: the urgent issue is not why they died but whether you will turn. Repentance is not mere regret but a reorientation of life toward God.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, kingship, reign. The kingdom of God begins as small as a mustard seed and as hidden as leaven in dough, yet it grows into a tree and works through the whole batch. Its beginnings look unimpressive, but its end fills the earth.

stenos.gloss: Narrow, confined. The door into the kingdom is narrow, and Jesus urges His hearers to strive to enter while it stands open. The narrowness is not stinginess but urgency: there is one way in, and the time to enter is now, before the master shuts the door.

teleioo.gloss: To complete, to finish, to bring to the goal. On the third day, Jesus says, He will be perfected, a word Luke uses for the completion of His mission in death and resurrection. The threats of Herod cannot hurry or halt a course fixed by God toward Jerusalem.

episynago.gloss: To gather together, as a hen gathers her chicks. The word carries the tenderness of God longing to shelter His people under His wings, and the grief of a love refused: how often I have longed to gather you, but you were unwilling.

basic.context: People bring Jesus news of a massacre, expecting Him to explain who deserved it. He refuses to rank the dead and instead warns everyone: unless you repent, you too will perish. He tells of a barren fig tree given one more year of patient care before it is cut down. On a Sabbath He frees a woman bent double for eighteen years, and when the synagogue leader objects, He exposes the hypocrisy of caring more for an ox than for a suffering daughter of Abraham. He pictures the kingdom as a mustard seed and as leaven, tiny beginnings that grow to fill everything. He warns that the door is narrow and will one day shut. And He weeps over Jerusalem, longing to gather her like a hen gathers her chicks, but she was unwilling.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the chapter as one sustained call to repent while there is still time. The disasters, the barren tree, and the narrow door all press the same urgency; the healing and the parables show the kingdom's grace and growth; the lament reveals the heart of God behind the warnings, grieving over a people who will not come.
basic.originalLanguages: The key verb is metanoeo, to repent, to turn the whole life around. The basileia, the kingdom, grows from the smallest seed. The door is stenos, narrow, and open only for now. Jesus says He will teleioo, be perfected, on the third day, His mission completed in death and resurrection. And He longs to episynago, to gather, His people as a hen gathers her chicks.
basic.typology: The barren fig tree recalls Israel as God's unfruitful vineyard, and looks ahead to the withered fig tree of Holy Week. The bent woman freed on the Sabbath pictures the true Sabbath rest Jesus brings. The hen gathering her chicks echoes the God who shelters His people under His wings in the Psalms.
basic.christ: Jesus is the patient keeper of the vineyard who pleads for one more year of grace, the Lord of the Sabbath who unbinds what Satan has bound, and the resolute Savior who cannot be scared off His road to Jerusalem by Herod the fox. Above all He is the God of Israel come in person, weeping over the city and longing to gather her children.
basic.god: God is patient, giving the barren tree another season; He is compassionate, freeing the bound even on His holy day; He is sovereign, growing His kingdom from nothing; and He is heartbroken love, longing to gather a people who refuse Him. His warnings and His tears come from the same heart.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 5 sings of Israel as a vineyard that yields no fruit. Psalm 91 speaks of finding refuge under God's wings. Matthew 7 sets the narrow gate against the wide road. Romans 2 warns that God's patience is meant to lead us to repentance.
basic.oneStory: As Jesus sets His face toward Jerusalem, He presses the whole nation, and every reader, to turn while the door stands open. The kingdom is quietly filling the earth, and the King Himself weeps over the city that will not be gathered, on His way to the cross that will open the door wide.

context.literary: The scene sits in Luke's long travel narrative toward Jerusalem, where teaching, controversy, and parable are woven together. The chapter is bracketed by the summons to repent and the lament over Jerusalem, both looking ahead to the city's judgment. The mustard seed and leaven pair as twin kingdom parables; the narrow door and the shut door press the theme of urgent entry.
context.historical: Pilate's slaughter of Galileans and the collapse of the tower of Siloam were recent tragedies that raised the ancient question of suffering and guilt. Sabbath healing was fiercely contested; the synagogue leader appeals to the six working days. The reference to Herod Antipas, called a fox, and to prophets perishing in Jerusalem, sets the political and prophetic stakes of Jesus' journey.
context.canonical: The barren fig tree and unfruitful vineyard draw on Israel's prophets, who charged the nation with yielding no fruit for God. The image of God gathering His people under His wings runs through the Psalms and Deuteronomy. The closing quotation, Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord, is Psalm 118, sung at the Triumphal Entry and pointing to Christ's return.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon every hearer to urgent repentance, to display the patience and compassion of God in the fig tree and the bent woman, to reveal the hidden but unstoppable growth of the kingdom, and to unveil the grief of the Savior over a city that will not come to Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The reports of disaster and the healing are narrated events, but Jesus draws from them a prescriptive call: repent, strive to enter, do not presume. The parables of the seed and the leaven describe the kingdom's nature; the lament reveals God's heart rather than commanding an action, yet it warns against the hardness that refuses His gathering love.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean by 'unless you repent, you too will all perish'?

views.label: A call to turn to God to escape final judgment
views.case: Jesus severs the link between particular tragedies and particular guilt, then universalizes the danger; the perishing in view is ultimate ruin under God's judgment, escaped only by genuine repentance. The barren fig tree that faces the axe confirms the stakes.

views.label: A warning of national catastrophe on Jerusalem
views.case: Some read the perishing as the coming destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70, when many Jews indeed perished; the lament over the city and its desolate house point that way. Yet this reading, while historically real, need not exclude the deeper and universal call to repentance.

debates.bearing: Whether one hears an ultimate or a historical warning, the summons is the same and it falls on us: turn to God now, for no one is exempt and the door will not stand open forever.

debates.question: Does the narrow door mean only a few will be saved?

views.label: The question is redirected from counting to entering
views.case: Asked whether few are saved, Jesus does not give a number but a command: make every effort to enter. He then pictures people streaming from every direction to the feast, so the emphasis falls not on scarcity but on the urgency of entering the one door while it is open.

views.label: The saved are genuinely few and the way is hard
views.case: The narrowness of the door and the warning that many will try and fail suggest that entry is costly and that presumption, We ate and drank with you, saves no one. The way is real and demanding, not a broad and easy path.

debates.bearing: Both guard against complacency: salvation is neither a matter of casual belonging nor of idle speculation, but of striving to enter through the one narrow door that is Christ Himself, while grace still holds it open.

study.originalLanguages: The governing verb is metanoeo, to repent, to change one's mind and turn the whole life toward God, pressed twice on the crowd who wanted to talk about other people's guilt. The basileia, the kingdom, is likened to a mustard seed and to leaven, hidden and small yet destined to fill the earth. The door into it is stenos, narrow, open now but soon to be shut. Jesus declares that on the third day He will teleioo, be brought to His goal, His mission completed in death and resurrection, a course no threat of Herod can alter. And over the city He longs to episynago, to gather her children as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, the tenderest picture of a love that Jerusalem refused.

typology.type: Israel as an unfruitful vineyard, Isaiah 5
typology.fulfillment: The barren fig tree in the vineyard, given one last season of grace before judgment, Luke 13:6-9.

typology.type: God sheltering His people under His wings, Psalm 91 and Deuteronomy 32
typology.fulfillment: Jesus longing to gather Jerusalem's children as a hen gathers her chicks, Luke 13:34.

typology.type: The Sabbath rest given to Israel
typology.fulfillment: The Lord of the Sabbath unbinding a daughter of Abraham on the Sabbath day, the true rest and release He brings, Luke 13:16.

study.christ: Jesus stands in this chapter as the patient Vinedresser pleading for one more year over the barren tree, the Lord of the Sabbath who loosens what Satan has bound, and the resolute Servant who will not let Herod the fox divert Him from His appointed road. When He says He will be perfected on the third day, He speaks of the death and resurrection that complete His mission. And in the lament He speaks as none but God can speak: how often I have longed to gather your children, the very voice of the covenant Lord grieving over His people, soon to enter the city on the words of Psalm 118 and, in the end, to be welcomed with them at His return.
study.god: The God revealed here is patient and compassionate, granting the fruitless tree another season and freeing a woman bound for eighteen years, even on His holy day. He is sovereign over history, planting a kingdom that grows unstoppably from the smallest seed. He is just, warning that the door will shut and that presumption will not save. And most movingly He is love that grieves, longing to shelter His people under His wings and heartbroken when they will not come. His warnings and His tears flow from one and the same heart.

crossReferences.note: The song of the vineyard that yields no fruit, the background to the barren fig tree.
He will cover you with His feathers, the sheltering wings Jesus longs to spread over Jerusalem.
The narrow gate and the hard way that leads to life, set against the broad road.
God's kindness and patience are meant to lead us to repentance, the truth behind the fig tree's reprieve.

study.oneStory: On the road to Jerusalem the King presses His people to turn while the door stands open, freeing the bound and planting a kingdom that will fill the earth from the smallest of seeds. Yet the city that kills its prophets will not be gathered, and so the Savior weeps, and goes on toward the cross where the barren tree will be spared, the narrow door thrown open, and the scattered children finally gathered under His wings.

quiz.q: How does Jesus respond to the news of the Galileans killed by Pilate?

options.text: He denies that the victims were worse sinners and calls everyone to repent or likewise perish.
options.note: Luke 13:2-5 refuses to rank guilt and turns the warning on all His hearers.

options.text: He warns that Jerusalem itself faces coming judgment.
options.note: True to the chapter's later lament, but here the direct point is the universal call to repent.

options.text: He explains the hidden sins that caused the tragedy.
options.note: Jesus does the opposite; He refuses to treat the disaster as a scoreboard of particular guilt.

options.text: He assures them the victims deserved exactly what they got.
options.note: He explicitly denies that they were worse sinners than anyone else.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the barren fig tree teach?

options.text: God grants a season of patient grace and tending, but fruitless life still faces judgment.
options.note: Luke 13:6-9 holds patience and warning together: one more year, then the axe.

options.text: God is patient and gives more time to bear fruit.
options.note: True, but it leaves out the warning that unfruitfulness finally meets judgment.

options.text: God will always spare the tree no matter what.
options.note: The reprieve is for one more year, not an unconditional guarantee.

options.text: Fruitfulness does not matter to God.
options.note: The whole point is that the owner comes looking for fruit and finds none.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus heal the bent woman on the Sabbath despite the objection?

options.text: Because a daughter of Abraham bound by Satan should surely be released on the Sabbath, the day of rest and freedom.
options.note: Luke 13:15-16 grounds the healing in the Sabbath's own meaning of release.

options.text: Because He is Lord of the Sabbath and exposes their hypocrisy.
options.note: True and implied, but the stated reason centers on the woman's release as a daughter of Abraham.

options.text: Because the Sabbath law did not really apply to healing.
options.note: Jesus does not dismiss the Sabbath; He reveals its true purpose of mercy and release.

options.text: Because the synagogue leader had given Him permission.
options.note: The leader was indignant and opposed the healing.

quiz.q: What do the mustard seed and the leaven reveal about the kingdom of God?

options.text: It begins small and hidden yet grows to fill and transform everything.
options.note: Luke 13:18-21 pairs tiny beginnings with pervasive, unstoppable growth.

options.text: It grows slowly over a long period of time.
options.note: Growth is real, but the point is the contrast between small beginnings and vast end.

options.text: It stays small and unnoticed in the world.
options.note: The opposite: the seed becomes a tree and the leaven fills the whole batch.

options.text: It arrives suddenly, full-grown, with no small beginning.
options.note: Both parables stress the smallness of the beginning.

quiz.q: What does Jesus' lament over Jerusalem reveal?

options.text: The grieving love of God who longed to gather His people, met by their unwillingness.
options.note: Luke 13:34 shows tender divine longing refused by the city.

options.text: That Jerusalem would be judged and left desolate.
options.note: True to verse 35, but the heart of the lament is God's grieving love, not only the judgment.

options.text: That Jesus was afraid of Herod's threats.
options.note: He calls Herod a fox and presses on resolutely; fear is not the point.

options.text: That God had given up on His people entirely.
options.note: The longing to gather and the hope of a future 'Blessed is He who comes' show enduring love.

questions: Jesus refuses to rank the Galileans as worse sinners and instead says, unless you repent. Where do you find yourself explaining other people's suffering rather than hearing God's call to turn?
The barren tree was given one more year of patient tending. Where has God been patient with you, and what fruit is He waiting to see?
Jesus freed a woman bent double for eighteen years and called her a daughter of Abraham. Is there a long-standing bondage you have stopped believing Christ could loosen?
The door is narrow and open now, but it will one day shut. What would it mean to make every effort to enter today rather than presuming on a later time?
Jesus wept over a city that would not be gathered. Where might you be resisting the sheltering love of God, unwilling to come under His wings?

translationNotes.note: A footnote records that several manuscripts read simply 'Cut it down' without the following clause; the sense is unchanged, the owner's patience has run out and judgment is due unless fruit appears.

The phrase rendered 'I will reach My goal' translates a Greek verb meaning to be completed or perfected. Luke uses it for the finishing of Jesus' God-appointed mission in His death and resurrection at Jerusalem, which no threat of Herod can hurry or prevent.
'Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord' quotes Psalm 118:26. It will be sung at the Triumphal Entry and points beyond it to the final recognition of Christ when He comes again.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 13:1-35. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text; no em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
